title: Tarte aux fraises
subtitle: French strawberry tart on a crème pâtissière base in a pâte sucrée case
excerpt: A classic French strawberry tart: a crisp sweet pastry case (pâte sucrée) blind-baked until golden, filled with a thick vanilla crème pâtissière, and topped with fresh strawberries glazed with warm apricot jam. Serves eight.
sourceNotes: The tarte aux fraises is documented in French patisserie from the nineteenth century as a seasonal preparation using the summer strawberry crop. Jules Gouffé, Le Livre de Pâtisserie (1873), public domain: a fruit tart made with a sweet pastry case filled with crème pâtissière and glazed fresh fruit is documented as a standard patisserie preparation. Urbain Dubois, La Cuisine Classique (1856), public domain: the combination of pâte sucrée, crème pâtissière, and fresh fruit with an apricot glaze is documented in the pastry chapter.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The pastry case and crème pâtissière can both be made a day ahead and kept separately in the fridge. Assemble no more than 2 hours before serving.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; blind bake 15 minutes with beans, then 8 minutes without until the base is biscuit-coloured and dry

glossaryTerms.definition: A French sweet shortcrust pastry made by creaming butter and icing sugar before adding eggs and flour. The creaming method produces a tender, biscuit-like crust that holds its shape cleanly when blind-baked. It is richer and sweeter than plain shortcrust and is used for tarts that will be filled with crème pâtissière or fresh fruit. Pâte sucrée is more delicate to handle than shortcrust: chill it thoroughly before rolling and after lining the tin to prevent shrinkage.

A thick, stirred custard set with cornflour and used as a filling for tarts, éclairs, and mille-feuille. Milk is heated to a simmer, then poured over a mixture of egg yolks, sugar, and cornflour; the mixture is returned to the heat and stirred until thick and smooth. A knob of butter stirred in off the heat gives gloss. The cream must be cooled quickly and stored with clingfilm pressed directly to the surface to prevent a skin forming.

content.text: case must be fully baked and completely cool before the
goes in; warmth will cause the cream to weep and the pastry to soften. Use the best strawberries available: the fruit is the tart.
Make the pâte sucrée and blind bake

Beat the softened {{unsalted-butter}} (pastry portion) and {{icing-sugar}} together until smooth. Beat in the {{eggs}} (pastry portion) and {{vanilla-extract}}. Add the {{plain-flour}} and mix until a soft dough forms. Flatten, wrap, and refrigerate for 30 minutes. Preheat the oven to 175°C fan. Roll the pastry to 3 mm thick and line the tart tin, pressing it into the fluted edges. Trim, prick the base, and refrigerate for 20 minutes. Line with paper and baking beans and blind bake for 15 minutes. Remove the beans and paper and bake for a further 8 minutes until the base is biscuit-coloured. Cool completely.
Make the crème pâtissière and cool

Heat the {{whole-milk}} with the {{vanilla-bean-paste}} until steaming. Whisk the {{eggs}} (crème portion), {{caster-sugar}}, and {{cornflour}} together in a bowl. Pour the hot milk over the egg mixture in a thin stream, whisking constantly. Return to the pan and stir over medium heat until very thick and bubbling. Remove from the heat and stir in the {{unsalted-butter}} (crème portion) until melted. Press clingfilm directly onto the surface and refrigerate for at least 30 minutes until cold and firm.

Spoon the cold crème pâtissière into the pastry case and smooth level with a palette knife. Arrange the {{strawberries}} over the cream in concentric circles or a pattern. Brush the {{jam}} (sieved, warmed to a liquid) lightly over the strawberries to glaze. Serve within 2 hours.
